input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A research‑oriented Living Lab building employed in the TwinLight and BOREALIS projects. The building contains a BIM‑based architectural model enriched with device definitions for lighting control and environmental monitoring, a programmable building‑control system (BCS) that executes real‑time control logic, and a dense network of lighting, occupancy, temperature and humidity sensors connected via an IoT gateway that streams data to the digital twin. The infrastructure supports continuous real‑time data exchange, immersive 3‑D visualization, and adaptive lighting experiments, making it an ideal testbed for demonstrating the full lifecycle of a digital twin—from model assembly and runtime integration to analytics and control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers multiple services:   • Monitoring of building subsystems (energy, temperature, occupancy) via real‑time telemetry.   • Prediction of future states and potential failures using time‑series analytics.   • Optimization of HVAC, lighting, and energy schedules to reduce operating costs.   • Direct control of actuators through command interfaces.   • Interactive visualization of building layout, status, and simulation results via dashboards.   • A plugin architecture that allows DT engineers to add custom analytic services. Target users are Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT engineers.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation employs a model‑driven engineering workflow that extracts structured representations from enriched BIM files and automatically generates deployable digital‑twin artifacts. Real‑time data exchange between the building’s IoT gateway and the twin is achieved through standard messaging protocols. Interactive visualization is delivered via a 3‑D rendering engine, and the runtime system is organized as a modular architecture with standardized extension points, enabling the integration of custom services, plugins, and analytics.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Continuous real‑time synchronization between the physical building and its digital twin is achieved through the framework’s real‑time data exchange capabilities. The system continuously pushes sensor measurements from the physical assets to the digital replica and pulls control commands back, ensuring a near‑zero‑lag loop. While explicit update frequencies or latency bounds are not specified in the documents, the architecture emphasizes continuous, low‑latency data flow to support live monitoring, predictive analytics, and rapid control decisions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Model‑driven deployment orchestrates an end‑to‑end lifecycle: enriched BIM data is parsed via MDE to generate structured digital twins for each physical component; a central DT framework hub orchestrates bidirectional data exchange, runtime configuration, and service plug‑in integration; the architecture follows a hub‑and‑spoke pattern with the central hub coordinating device‑level sub‑twin instances, encapsulating device modeling, control logic, and monitoring; resource management and coordination are handled by this central component, while DT Engineers extend functionality through standardized extension points.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The platform integrates with external BIM tools for device modeling and with enterprise or building‑management systems through standardized extension points and plugin interfaces. Interaction with other Digital Twins or external systems is mediated by the framework’s modular architecture, which allows developers to plug in adapters for data exchange, authentication, and orchestration. Although specific communication protocols are not named, the design supports interoperability across heterogeneous systems, enabling the DT to serve as a bridge between the static BIM representation and dynamic operational environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are derived from enriched BIM models that embed sensors, actuators and control logic directly within Revit/IFC representations. The model‑driven deployment framework extracts structured semantic data via MDE principles, feeding the resulting artifacts through a validation component that enforces value ranges and units via Eclipse Ditto’s policy‑based engine. Device configurations are then transformed into deployable Twin artifacts fed into a middleware stack consisting of Eclipse Hono (providing at‑least‑once message delivery) and Eclipse Ditto (providing persistent event storage and policy enforcement). The twin’s runtime representation is instantiated in UE5, with LightTwinComponent, HVACFeatureParser, and generic TwinComponent modules mapping BIM attributes to scene objects. Extension points (TwinComponent and FeatureParser) support the integration of custom services, while the Hono–Ditto pipeline ensures real‑time, bi‑directional synchronicity between the physical building and its digital counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation logic is defined by building control engineers within the BIM model. The prototype demonstrates a lighting actuation path via the LightTwinComponent, which brokers front‑end commands through the UnrealTwinController to DittoController and DittoDevice, ultimately translating brightness and colour‑temperature (in Kelvin) changes to the physical LED fixtures. Actuator commands are processed through a policy validator that enforces per‑device value ranges and unit consistency, thereby preventing unsafe operations. Control messages travel over the Eclipse Hono message broker, typically using MQTT payloads, allowing remote command issuance from the UE5 user interface to the physical actuator fleet.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing devices are embedded in BIM with explicit metadata (type, measurement range). They transmit data via Hono to Ditto; Ditto enforces policy validation for ranges and units. A 1 Hz heartbeat coordinates state exchange, achieving near‑instantaneous state reflection. Persistent event storage guarantees durability of sensor transmissions.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model and data fidelity is enforced through several mechanisms. The deployment pipeline validates structural consistency during the Model‑Checking and Model‑Parsing stages. Runtime validity is verified by Ditto’s policy engine, ensuring value ranges and units, and by Hono’s at‑least‑once delivery guaranteeing no message loss. The Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric quantifies entanglement via its components: timeliness (0.997, indicating minimal delay across the 1 s heartbeat), reliability (1.0, no lost updates), and availability (1.0, full uptime), yielding an ODTE score of 0.997 over a 12‑minute measurement window. These metrics, together with persistent event storage and policy validation, provide rigorous quality assurance for the twin’s synchronization with the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real‑time data integration is achieved through Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto, enabling continuous synchronization between physical assets and their digital twins. Sensor telemetry and device status updates are transmitted from buildings to the twin in a near‑real‑time, event‑driven manner, allowing the enriched BIM models to be updated post‑deployment and to maintain semantic consistency.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Digital Twin (e.g., HVAC adjustments, lighting changes) are sent through the Hono/Ditto messaging stack to the building control systems, enabling bidirectional communication and confirmation of command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin middleware employs Eclipse Hono as a message broker to ingest data from sensors and control devices, and Eclipse Ditto to manage the digital twin representation. This stack delivers interoperability across heterogeneous systems and provides the required real‑time connectivity between physical and virtual layers.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle follows a model‑driven deployment workflow that begins with an enriched Building Information Model (BIM) where Building Control Engineers directly embed sensors, actuators and control logic into the 3‑D representation. Using Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles, the enriched BIM is automatically extracted into a structured runtime artefact, and a reproducible deployment pipeline (R2) deploys the digital twin with minimal manual intervention. During operation, live telemetry from the physical building is streamed via Eclipse Hono to the twin service (Eclipse Ditto) for continuous synchronization, while facility managers interact with the twin through interactive visualization interfaces. Lifecycle governance is defined by the role hierarchy in Table I: Building Control Engineers manage model creation, DT Engineers extend functionality, and Facility Managers oversee runtime monitoring and control, ensuring that each phase—design, deployment, runtime, and evolution—is coordinated under a formally defined change‑management process.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering process is fully model‑driven: Building Control Engineers create enriched BIM models with device metadata, which the framework transforms into structured artefacts via MDE. The transformation pipeline guarantees consistent, reproducible runtime artefacts (requiring minimal manual configuration). Modularity (R4) defines explicit extension points for domain‑specific services or analytics, allowing DT Engineers to evolve the twin by adding plug‑in components without disrupting the core runtime. Evolution management is supported through the framework’s automated deployment process (R2) and the modular architecture, which isolates changes and facilitates iterative development.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real‑time data integration (R3) provides continuous synchronization of sensor and actuator data between the physical building and its Digital Twin via Eclipse Hono and Ditto. Interactive visualization interfaces, described as immersive interfaces for monitoring and control, allow Facility Managers to observe current building state, view device performance, and issue control commands directly from the twin. The decision‑support capability focuses on live situational awareness and direct control operations; confidence in the data is maintained through live bidirectional synchronization rather than predictive or statistical models.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The architecture adheres to open‑source, industry‑standard protocols: MQTT for low‑latency messaging through Eclipse Hono, JSON and REST APIs for twin manipulation via Eclipse Ditto, and the semantic integration promised by the MDE transformation rules. It explicitly addresses the documented lack of standardized data schemas by generating compliant Ditto twin definitions from enriched BIM models, thereby conforming to JSON‑LD‑style structures. The framework’s modular design (R4) permits the adoption of additional interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, BACnet) through plug‑in extension points, while still relying on the core Eclipse stack for connectivity and device management.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the Living Lab context, the TwinLight and BOREALIS projects, and the integration of BIM with building control systems (BCS). It correctly identifies the focus on real-time data exchange, 3-D visualization, and adaptive lighting experiments. However, it lists specific environmental sensors ('temperature and humidity') that are not explicitly mentioned in the provided snippets, though 'sensor configurations' are.

The extraction is excellent and fully grounded in the source text. It accurately lists the services (monitoring, prediction, optimization, control, visualization, plugin architecture) and correctly identifies the target users (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) as defined in Table I and the abstract.

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate description of the model-driven engineering workflow, including the extraction of semantics from BIM, automatic generation of artifacts, and the use of standard messaging protocols for real-time data. It correctly describes the modular architecture and extension points. It omits the specific tool names (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine) which are present in the source, preventing a perfect score.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework

DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_2_judge.reasoning:
The extraction accurately describes the system's temporal synchronization based on the source text. It correctly identifies the 'real-time data exchange' and 'live synchronization' capabilities, and honestly notes that specific latency metrics are not provided in the text, while inferring the continuous nature of the loop from the bidirectional communication requirements.

Quotes: 'real-time data exchange', 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins', 'continuous synchronization'.

The provided text describes a framework for deploying Digital Twins and managing 'twinnable devices', but does not define the 'multiplicities' characteristic in terms of hierarchical structures of multiple distinct Digital Twin instances or their coordination. The extraction correctly states this is 'Not in Document'.

The extraction accurately captures the orchestration flow, describing 'Model‑driven deployment' that parses 'enriched BIM data' via MDE and a 'central DT framework hub' for 'bidirectional data exchange'. This aligns with source quotes like 'model-driven framework,' 'automates the extraction... generation of runtime interfaces,' and 'DT Framework acts as a central hub.' Some specific terms like 'hub‑and‑spoke' are reasonable inferences from the 'central hub' description.

The extraction provides a good summary of the integration capabilities, correctly identifying the use of BIM tools for modeling and 'standardized extension points' for modularity. It accurately reflects the framework's role as a 'bridge' between static and dynamic environments. It adds minor details like 'authentication' and interaction with 'other Digital Twins' which are not explicitly in the text, but these align with the general description of interoperability.

Quotes: 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'bridges the gap between design-time models and runtime operations', 'interoperability'.

V. R ESULTS AND D ISCUSSION

We have evaluated our approach in the context of a Living
Lab [75] at the University of Innsbruck. Figure 4a shows the
BIM model of the Living Lab, which comprises an office space
of 22 m [2], two working places, as well as various sensors and

Fig. 4: Visualization of the Living Lab: (a) BIM model in Revit (b) 3D rendering in UE5.



TABLE VI: Deployment Times for Digital Twin


**Stage** **Duration** (seconds)


Model Checking 5.53
Model Parsing 8.7
Initialization and Setup 0.3


in 0.3 seconds. This considerably short deployment time of
14.53 seconds underscores the efficiency of our proposal in
streamlining the DT creation process. Our deployment was
done on a standard

The Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) [18] metric is a crucial measure of the synchronization quality between DTs and their physical counterparts. It is composed of
three main components: timeliness, reliability, and availability.
_Timeliness_ ( _T_ ) reflects how up-to-date the data is that the
DT receives, measuring the delay between a state change
occurring in the physical counterpart and the subsequent
update in the DT. _Reliability_ ( _R_ ) measures the consistency
in communication between the physical counterpart and the
DT, defined as the ratio of the number of state updates
successfully received by the DT to the number of updates
expected. _Availability_ ( _A_ ) assesses the operational uptime of
the physical counterpart as perceived by the DT, measuring
the extent to which the physical counterpart is accessible and
capable of sending updates. The ODTE score then is calculated
as the product of these components (cf. _ODTE_ = _T ×R×A_ ),
providing a comprehensive assessment of system performance.
Table VII shows our results.

In our assessment, the scores were 0.997 for timeliness
(indicating some slight delay in communication), 1.0 for
reliability (no message loss), and 1.0 for availability (100%
up-time) resulting in an overall ODTE value of 0.997. We
measured the ODTE metric over a continuous 12-minute



TABLE VII: ODTE Measurements


**ODTE Component** **Score**


Timeliness 0.997
Reliability 1.0
Availability 1.0


**Total Score** 0.997


measurement window. The setup employed a regular 1-second
heartbeat mechanism to coordinate data exchange and state
updates between the physical and digital twin. Throughout
the experiment, the DT maintained a consistent and exact
reflection of the physical system’s state, with no observable
divergence in state, reflecting ideal entanglement under the
given conditions. Our experiments assess bidirectional synchronization by transmitting lighting control commands from
the DT to the physical system and verifying the corresponding
state updates in the reverse direction. The absence of such
updates would indicate a communication failure, resulting in
a total ODTE score of 0, thereby validating the integrity of
the bidirectional communication channel.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

[64]. Requirements regarding the complexity of construction
projects and availability over long building life cycles are
covered by BIM. It is therefore essential that concepts such as
DTs in the construction sector are also designed for longevity
and compatibility with existing, AECO-specific systems and
standards such as BIM.
Despite these strengths, BIM however exhibits significant
limitations when considering its application as a foundation
for DTs [7], [9], [14]. First, BIM models are predominantly
static representations focused on design and construction
phases, lacking mechanisms for real-time data integration from
operational buildings. Second, BIM tools typically provide
limited support for bidirectional communication with physical
systems, a core requirement for functional DTs. Third, while
BIM excels at representing physical and spatial aspects of
buildings, it lacks native capabilities for modeling runtime
aspects of Building Control Systems (BCS), essential for
operational monitoring and control [7], [11], [12], [21].
These limitations create a fundamental gap between BIM’s
design-time focus and the operational requirements of DTs.
DTs require continuous synchronization with physical assets
through sensor data, the ability to process and analyze this
data in real-time, and mechanisms to feed control signals
back to the BCS. In addition, DTs also require extensible
architectures that can accommodate evolving services and
analytics throughout the building life cycle [7].
Nevertheless, BIM provides an ideal semantic foundation
for DTs due to its comprehensive representation of building


geometry, topology, and component relationships. What is
needed is a systematic approach to transform these static
BIM models into dynamic, operational DTs by addressing the
identified limitations and extending BIM through a formal
methodology for modeling BCS runtime aspects and their
integration with a building’s digital representation.


_A. Uses Cases, Requirements, and Solution Proposal_


TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
